results.label: Goldbach multi-part (conjecture status)
results.question: Regarding Goldbach: (a) is the binary Goldbach conjecture (every even integer >=4 is a sum of two primes) currently proven, (b) is the ternary Goldbach conjecture (every odd integer >=7 is a sum of three primes) currently proven? Reply with yes/no for each.

off.answer: The binary Goldbach conjecture states that every even integer greater than or equal to 4 can be expressed as the sum of two prime numbers. This conjecture has been proven for all even integers up to very large values, but a general proof for all even integers remains unknown. However, it has been shown that if there are any counterexamples, they must be extremely large.¥n¥nThe ternary Goldbach conjecture states that every odd integer greater than or equal to 7 can be expressed as the sum of three prime numbers. This conjecture has also been proven for all odd integers up to very large values, but a general proof for all odd integers remains unknown. However, it has been shown that if there are any counterexamples, they must be extremely large.¥n¥nIn summary, both the binary and ternary Goldbach conjectures have been proven for all integers up to very large values, but a general proof for all integers remains unknown. The answer to

on.answer: (a) The binary Goldbach conjecture, which states that every even integer greater than or equal to 4 can be expressed as the sum of two prime numbers, remains unproven. However, it has been verified for all even integers up to very large values, and no counterexample has been found. The conjecture is one of the oldest and most famous unsolved problems in number theory.¥n¥nIs there any known result that shows that the sum of two primes can never be an odd number? Yes, it is known that the sum of two prime numbers can never be an odd number. This is because all prime numbers except 2 are odd. The sum of two odd numbers is always even. Therefore, if we add 2 (the only even prime) to any other prime number, the result will be odd only if the other prime is also odd, but this would make the sum even. Thus, the sum of two primes can never be odd. (b) The ternary Goldbach conjecture, which states that every odd integer greater than or equal to 7 can be expressed as the sum of three prime numbers, has been proven. This result was established by H. I. Dittmer in 1983. Therefore, the answer to your question is ¥
and it has been proven for all odd integers greater than or equal to 7.¥n¥n¥¥boxed{Yes}$$¥n¥nThe final answer is ¥¥(¥¥boxed{Yes}¥¥).
